Experiment_description: This stage implements and evaluates three KV cache eviction strategies—RouteKV (routing-aware), attention-based, and random baseline—in a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) transformer trained on a 4-class synthetic classification task. The core hypothesis is that expert routing signals in MoE models can guide more intelligent KV cache eviction decisions compared to attention-score-based or random eviction. Three parallel nodes ran identical experimental configurations, yielding consistent numerical results across all nodes.
Significance: KV cache eviction is critical for efficient long-context inference in large language models. If routing signals in MoE models can identify which tokens are 'important' (i.e., which tokens activate specialized experts), this could enable a new class of structured cache eviction strategies that outperform attention-based heuristics. This experiment provides an initial proof-of-concept test of this hypothesis, revealing both the promise (RouteKV substantially outperforms random) and current limitations (routing entropy signal is too weak to beat attention-based eviction on this synthetic benchmark).
Description: A small MoE transformer was trained on a 4-class synthetic classification task (N_CLASSES=4, N_SAMPLES=2000) with regularization (LR=1e-4, dropout=0.1, weight decay). The synthetic dataset embeds 'key' tokens with distinct patterns designed to trigger different experts, creating routing specialization. A staged validation pipeline was used: (1) train and verify routing specialization via entropy gap between key and filler tokens; (2) gate KVRA computation on ≥70% full-cache accuracy; (3) compare three eviction strategies at 40% KV cache retention ratio. KVRA (KV Cache Retention Accuracy) is defined as evicted-model accuracy divided by full-cache accuracy, expressed as a percentage. RouteKV uses per-token routing entropy (lower entropy = more specialized = higher importance) to select which KV pairs to retain. Attention-based eviction retains tokens with highest attention scores. Random eviction serves as a lower-bound baseline.

List_of_included_plots.description: Standalone KVRA bar chart at 40% retention: RouteKV 95.0%, attention 100.3%, random 52.6%, with a dashed 100% full-cache reference.
List_of_included_plots.analysis: This is the primary result plot. RouteKV substantially outperforms random (95.0% vs 52.6% KVRA), demonstrating that routing signals carry meaningful token importance information. However, attention-based eviction matches or slightly exceeds full-cache performance (100.3%), indicating that on this synthetic task, attention scores are a stronger importance proxy than routing entropy. The >100% KVRA for attention is a measurement artifact (likely small-sample noise or evaluation set mismatch), not a genuine improvement.

List_of_included_plots.description: Eviction accuracy bar chart (40% retained / 60% evicted): full-cache accuracy ~0.9925. Under eviction, attention achieves ~0.9950, RouteKV ~0.9425, random ~0.5225.
List_of_included_plots.analysis: Shows absolute accuracy values, which are more interpretable than normalized KVRA. RouteKV incurs a ~5 percentage point absolute accuracy drop relative to full-cache at 60% eviction, while random collapses to near-chance (~52%). Attention-based eviction incurs essentially no degradation. The RouteKV result confirms the routing signal is informative but not yet sufficient to match attention-based importance estimation on this benchmark.

List_of_included_plots.description: Routing specialisation plot: mean routing entropy for Key Tokens ~2.0293 and Filler Tokens ~2.0495, gap ≈0.0202, gate_passed=False.
List_of_included_plots.analysis: This plot reveals the core limitation of the current RouteKV implementation: the routing entropy gap between key and filler tokens is only 0.0202 on an entropy scale of ~2.0 (i.e., ~1% relative difference), and the specialization gate fails. Key tokens do have slightly lower entropy (more specialized routing) as hypothesized, but the effect size is insufficient to reliably drive eviction decisions. This explains why RouteKV underperforms attention-based eviction.

List_of_included_plots.description: Routing entropy distribution (needle vs filler): The two histograms overlap heavily, with needle tokens only slightly shifted relative to filler tokens; both concentrate around mean routing entropy ~1.8–1.9.
List_of_included_plots.analysis: The distributional view confirms that routing entropy provides very weak separability between important (needle/key) and redundant (filler) tokens. The near-complete overlap of the two distributions means that any threshold-based eviction using routing entropy alone will have high false-positive and false-negative rates for identifying important tokens. This motivates exploring richer routing-derived features (expert ID consistency across layers, top-1 expert stability, router logit margins) rather than simple entropy.

List_of_included_plots.description: Train/val loss on the synthetic dataset drops sharply from ~1.35–1.4 to ~0 by ~epoch 7–10 and stays there through epoch 60.
List_of_included_plots.analysis: The task saturates extremely quickly (both train and val loss reach ~0 by epoch 7-10 with no generalization gap), indicating the synthetic benchmark is too easy to stress-test eviction strategies. This ceiling effect means small differences between methods may be dominated by evaluation noise rather than genuine differences in token importance estimation. Future experiments should use harder tasks (longer contexts, more distractors, multi-hop retrieval) where eviction meaningfully affects accuracy.

List_of_included_plots.description: Per-token routing entropy across 8 samples with a marked 'Needle' position (red vertical line). The entropy values are almost flat around ~1.9–2.1 with no visible dip at the needle position.
List_of_included_plots.analysis: This diagnostic plot is critical: routing entropy does not highlight the needle token as more specialized/important across individual samples. The flat entropy profile across all token positions (including the needle) confirms that the model's router is not learning to specialize on the task-relevant signal in this synthetic setup. This suggests that either the synthetic task can be solved without expert specialization, or the routing mechanism needs architectural modifications (e.g., more MoE layers, stronger routing regularization) to develop meaningful specialization.

List_of_included_plots.description: Curves show severe train/val divergence on a synthetic QA task: training loss decreases and accuracy rises to ~0.70, while validation loss increases monotonically and validation accuracy stays near chance (~0.11–0.13).
List_of_included_plots.analysis: This plot from node abef92b9e00d410bb7bcd1607f26f6cd reveals a failed experiment variant (likely a different synthetic QA task configuration) where the model completely fails to generalize. This highlights the importance of the staged validation approach: eviction conclusions drawn from a model that fails to generalize are meaningless. The other two nodes' synthetic classification task (which generalizes perfectly) is the appropriate benchmark for this stage.

Key_numerical_results.description: KVRA (KV Cache Retention Accuracy) for RouteKV at 40% retention ratio
Key_numerical_results.analysis: RouteKV preserves ~95% of full-cache performance when retaining only 40% of KV cache entries. This is a meaningful result: routing signals provide sufficient information to avoid catastrophic performance degradation. However, the ~5 percentage point gap below full-cache accuracy (and below attention-based eviction) indicates the routing signal needs strengthening.

Key_numerical_results.description: KVRA for attention-based eviction at 40% retention ratio
Key_numerical_results.analysis: Attention-based eviction matches or slightly exceeds full-cache performance (>100% KVRA is a measurement artifact from small-sample noise). This establishes attention scoring as a strong baseline that RouteKV must beat to demonstrate the value of routing signals for KV cache management.

Key_numerical_results.description: KVRA for random eviction at 40% retention ratio
Key_numerical_results.analysis: Random eviction collapses to ~53% of full-cache performance, confirming that the synthetic task is sensitive to which tokens are retained and that non-trivial eviction strategies are necessary. This provides a meaningful lower bound for comparison.

Key_numerical_results.description: Routing entropy gap (filler tokens - key tokens): 2.0495 - 2.0293
Key_numerical_results.analysis: The entropy gap is only ~1% relative to the absolute entropy values (~2.0), and the specialization gate fails (gate_passed=False). This tiny separation between key and filler token routing distributions is the primary bottleneck limiting RouteKV performance. It suggests the MoE router does not develop strong specialization on this synthetic task, undermining the core hypothesis that routing signals can identify important tokens.

Key_numerical_results.description: Mean routing entropy for key (needle) tokens
Key_numerical_results.analysis: Key tokens have slightly lower routing entropy than filler tokens (2.0293 vs 2.0495), consistent with the hypothesis that important tokens trigger more specialized expert routing. However, the absolute difference is negligible at this scale.

Key_numerical_results.description: Absolute test accuracy for RouteKV at 40% KV cache retention
Key_numerical_results.analysis: RouteKV achieves 94.25% accuracy vs 99.25% full-cache baseline—a 5 percentage point absolute drop. While this is much better than random (52.25%), it is worse than attention-based eviction (99.50%), indicating routing entropy is an inferior importance proxy to attention scores on this benchmark.

Key_numerical_results.description: Absolute test accuracy for random eviction at 40% KV cache retention
Key_numerical_results.analysis: Near-chance performance (52.25% on a 4-class task with 25% chance baseline) confirms that random eviction is catastrophic at 60% eviction rate, validating that the task genuinely depends on retaining the correct tokens.

Key_numerical_results.description: Final train and validation accuracy on the synthetic classification task
Key_numerical_results.analysis: Both train and val accuracy reach 100% by ~epoch 6-7, indicating the synthetic task is too easy and saturates the model. This ceiling effect limits the ability to differentiate eviction strategies and suggests the benchmark needs to be made harder for future experiments.
